1.synthesis:
The book of Leviticus concludes not with another law of impurity or sacrifice but with the voluntary: vows. A person who dedicates himself — or another person under his care — to the LORD by a special vow must pay a monetary equivalent to the sanctuary, with the valuation set according to age and sex. Matthew Henry introduces the section with characteristic generosity: "We should not only ask, What must we do, but, What may we do, for the glory and honour of God? As the liberal devises liberal things, so the pious devises pious things, and the enlarged heart would willingly do something extraordinary in the service of so good a Master as God is."

The valuations follow a careful grid (vv. 3-7): adult males 50 shekels, females 30; young men 20, girls 10; male infants 5, female 3; elderly men 15, women 10. Keil and Delitzsch observe that the valuations are calibrated to "capacity and vigour of life," the female appraised at approximately half the male — a reflection not of personal worth but of productive capacity in an agricultural economy where the vow-payment functioned as a sanctuary contribution. The provision for those too poor to pay the fixed rate (v. 8) ensures that poverty cannot prevent covenant participation: the priest sets an individually affordable valuation, making the entire system accessible regardless of economic standing.
1.historical_context: Vows in ancient Israel were solemn and legally binding (Num 30:2; Deut 23:21-23). A vow to dedicate a person typically meant dedicating them to Levitical or sanctuary service, as Hannah dedicated Samuel (1 Sam 1:11). The monetary commutation system allowed those who could not practically serve in the sanctuary to fulfill their vow through payment. Keil and Delitzsch note that haphläh — making a "special" or "singular" vow — was regarded as an extraordinary act of piety beyond what the law required, a freewill expression of devotion that God welcomed and regulated.
1.literary_structure: Verses 1-2 introduce the category (persons vowed to the LORD), verses 3-7 provide the valuation tables in descending order: peak-productive adults (20-60), then youth (5-20), then infancy (under 5), then old age (over 60), with male and female values at each level. Verse 8 gives the poverty exception, which ensures no Israelite is excluded by inability to pay. The movement from the general command to the detailed table to the pastoral exception shows the law's care both for precision and for inclusion.
1.theological_application: Gill emphasizes the grace extended to the poor in verse 8: "if he is too poor for the valuation, then he shall present himself before the priest, and the priest shall value him" — no Israelite was excluded from vow-fulfillment by poverty alone. Matthew Henry draws a spiritual lesson: every believer owes God more than they can repay, and Christ has made the ultimate payment; believers bring what they can, and divine grace makes up the difference. The voluntary character of these vows — the law does not require them but honors and regulates them — reflects the freewill heart of true worship: God receives with pleasure what is freely offered from a grateful heart (2 Cor 9:7).
1.christology: The vow of a person to the LORD is the shadow of which the incarnation is the substance: the eternal Son freely dedicates himself to the Father's will — "Behold, I have come to do your will, O God" (Heb 10:7, citing Ps 40:6-8). As the vow-payer brings what can be assessed and offered, Christ brings what is beyond all valuation: "the precious blood of Christ, like that of a lamb without blemish or spot" (1 Pet 1:19). The poverty provision — "if he is too poor for the valuation" — anticipates the gospel offer to those who have nothing to bring: Christ's worth is credited to the one who can offer no adequate payment of their own (Rom 4:5).

9.synthesis:
When the vow involves an animal suitable for sacrifice — a clean animal — the dedication is absolute and immediate: "whatever anyone gives to the LORD is holy" (v. 9). Once given, the animal passes entirely from human control; no substitution is permitted. If the offerer attempts to swap a better animal for a lesser — or a lesser for a better — both animals become holy, forfeited entirely to the sanctuary. Keil and Delitzsch note that this rule prevented the temptation to make a vow impulsively in a moment of devotion and then bargain down when the cost became clear. The absolute irrevocability of the gift reflects the sacredness of what is given to the holy: once something enters God's possession, it cannot be recalled.

Verses 11-13 deal with unclean animals — those ineligible for sacrifice such as donkeys or camels — which have economic value that could serve the sanctuary through sale. For these, the priest sets a valuation, and the original offerer may redeem the animal by paying the assessed price plus one-fifth (a twenty percent premium). If the offerer declines to redeem, the animal is sold at the priest's valuation. Matthew Henry observes that the added fifth serves as a discouragement to hasty vowing: "It is better not to vow, than to vow and not to pay" (Eccl 5:5). The premium makes the cost of retrieving a gift to God higher than the original cost of keeping it — a built-in incentive toward counted-cost deliberation before any vow is made.
9.historical_context: Keil and Delitzsch explain that the absolute irrevocability of a vowed clean animal reflects a principle seen throughout the ancient Near East: what has been consecrated to a deity enters the divine domain and cannot be reclaimed. In Israel this principle is theologically grounded rather than merely customary: the animal becomes holy because God is holy, and holiness is not a revocable quality. The unclean animal provision shows the system's practicality: the sanctuary benefited financially from animals that could not be offered on the altar but could be sold, their value supporting the Levitical ministry.
9.literary_structure: Verses 9-10 govern clean animals (absolute dedication, no substitution, both animals become holy if substitution is attempted). Verses 11-13 govern unclean animals (priest's valuation, optional redemption at +20%, or sale at assessed value). The section is compact but precise: two categories, two rules, with the twenty-percent redemption premium appearing here as it does throughout the chapter as a consistent principle distinguishing gift-to-God from ordinary commerce.
9.theological_application: Gill draws a spiritual lesson from the substitution prohibition: when we have given something to God — whether time, resources, or a commitment — we dishonor him by substituting something inferior or reclaiming what we offered. Matthew Henry applies this to Christian vows and commitments broadly: "Men's own estimation and computation of their vow are not to be taken; the judgment of the spiritual court must pass upon them." The priest's role in valuing the unclean animal suggests that the measurement of what is given to God is not left to the giver's wishful calculation but requires honest assessment against an objective standard.
9.christology: The rule that "whatever anyone gives to the LORD is holy" points to the logic of consecration in Christ: those given to the Son by the Father cannot be lost — "All that the Father gives me will come to me, and whoever comes to me I will never cast out" (John 6:37). The irrevocability of a vowed animal pictures the security of the redeemed. The absolute holiness conferred on the vowed animal also anticipates the New Testament's language of believers as "holy ones" — set apart not by their own merit but by the consecrating act of God through Christ, who "has perfected for all time those who are being sanctified" (Heb 10:14).

14.synthesis:
The dedication legislation extends from persons and animals to property: a man may devote his house or a portion of his field to the LORD, with the priest setting the valuation. Both may be redeemed — a house by paying the assessed value plus one-fifth, ancestral land by a calculation based on its barley-seed capacity and the remaining years to the next jubilee. Keil and Delitzsch note that the land-valuation formula — "fifty shekels of silver for a homer of barley seed" (v. 16) — tied the dedication's value directly to agricultural productivity and the jubilee countdown, integrating vow-law tightly with the sabbatical-year system of the preceding chapter.

If the man who dedicated a field later sells it to another person before redeeming it himself, the land cannot revert to him at jubilee; it becomes permanently holy to the LORD (v. 21), handed over to the priests. This provision was a deliberate safeguard against using sanctuary-dedication as a legal maneuver to complicate land transactions or evade the jubilee-reversion obligations of Leviticus 25. Verses 22-25 handle the separate case of purchased land — land a man has bought rather than inherited — which follows a different calculation since it will revert to the original family at jubilee regardless. Verse 25 specifies the "shekel of the sanctuary" as the authoritative unit of measurement throughout.
14.historical_context: Leviticus 27:14-25 presupposes the land-tenure system of Leviticus 25: all Israelite land is held in tribal trust with jubilee reversion, and the valuation of dedicated land accounts for the jubilee. Matthew Henry notes that the distinction between ancestral land (v. 16) and purchased land (v. 22) is significant because purchased land already has a jubilee reversion built in — it goes back to the original family, not the buyer — so its dedication-value must reflect the more limited tenure that the buyer actually holds. The integration of vow-law with jubilee-law makes the entire Levitical economic system mutually reinforcing.
14.literary_structure: Verses 14-15 handle dedicated houses (priest's valuation; redeemable at +20%). Verses 16-21 handle ancestral field dedication (valued by barley-seed capacity proportional to jubilee; forfeited permanently if sold rather than redeemed; becomes priestly property at jubilee). Verses 22-25 handle purchased-field dedication (different valuation; reverts to original owner at jubilee, not to the dedicator). Verse 25 closes with the standardization of currency. The movement is from immovable property type to immovable property type, each with its own jubilee-adjusted rule.
14.theological_application: Gill finds in the dedication of houses and land a model of comprehensive stewardship: not only time and persons but material property itself may be consecrated to God, and the law honors this impulse while governing it carefully. Matthew Henry sees the jubilee-adjustment in land valuation as a built-in check against both manipulation and overvaluation: "God consults the good of the sanctuary no less than the interest of the worshippers." The forfeiture of a field that is sold before redemption (vv. 20-21) teaches that gifts to God, once offered, cannot be redirected to personal advantage without permanent cost — the field goes to the priests, not back to the original owner.
14.christology: The house dedicated to the LORD anticipates the church as "God's household" (Eph 2:19), built on Christ the cornerstone. The precision of the jubilee-adjusted land valuation — worth declining as jubilee approaches — mirrors the New Testament's eschatological perspective: "the time is short" (1 Cor 7:29), and what is offered to God now carries eternal weight disproportionate to its temporal duration. The permanent holiness of an unredeemed dedicated field (v. 21) points to the irreversible consecration of all things at the consummation, when "the kingdoms of this world have become the kingdoms of our Lord and of his Christ" (Rev 11:15).

26.synthesis:
Two categories lie entirely outside the ordinary vow system because they already belong to God by prior claim. The firstborn of clean animals cannot be vowed, because the firstborn already belongs to the LORD and is therefore not the owner's to offer (v. 26; cf. Num 18:15-17). An unclean firstborn may be redeemed at priest's valuation plus one-fifth, or sold if not redeemed. The devoted thing (ḥērem) is even more severe: what is placed under absolute ban "shall not be sold or redeemed" and a person placed under ḥērem "shall surely be put to death" (v. 29).

Keil and Delitzsch note that the ḥērem represents the most extreme form of consecration, removing something from human use entirely and consigning it irreversibly to God — either destroyed utterly (as in holy-war application) or given permanently to the priests. Matthew Henry observes that this provision protected the vow system from inflation: if everything could be dedicated and then redeemed for a price, the concept of absolute gift to God would be emptied of meaning. The ḥērem preserves a category of genuine irrevocability, a gift so total that no subsequent transaction can reach it.
26.historical_context: The concept of ḥērem appears prominently in the conquest narrative: Jericho and its contents were placed under ḥērem (Josh 6:17-19), and Achan's violation of the ban cost him his life (Josh 7:1). The ban on vowing firstborn clean animals parallels the firstborn legislation in Numbers 18: the LORD's claim on the firstborn was established at the Exodus, when God spared Israel's firstborn and claimed them as his own (Exod 13:2). They belong to him by redemptive act, not human offering — they were his before any vow was made.
26.literary_structure: Verses 26-27 address firstborn animals: clean ones cannot be vowed (already belong to God); unclean ones may be redeemed at +20% or sold. Verses 28-29 address the devoted thing (ḥērem): a sharply different category where absolute irrevocability applies to both objects and persons placed under the ban. The contrast between the firstborn (belonging to God by redemptive claim) and the ḥērem (dedicated by human vow to absolute destruction) shows the two ends of the consecration spectrum: prior divine claim versus maximum human consecration, both resulting in what cannot be retrieved.
26.theological_application: Gill draws attention to the graduated nature of consecration throughout this chapter: ordinary vows, dedicated things, and the absolute ḥērem — each category is more binding than the last, and the chapter's final sections address what lies entirely beyond redemption. The principle that the firstborn already belongs to God — before any human act of offering — teaches that certain gifts precede human initiative: God has a prior claim on the firstborn of every creature. Matthew Henry applies this to the Christian: God chose us before we chose him (John 15:16), and the dedication of our lives is not an independent act but a response to a prior divine claim and love.
26.christology: The firstborn legislation points unmistakably to Christ as "the firstborn of all creation" (Col 1:15) and "the firstborn from the dead" (Col 1:18; Rev 1:5) — the one who belongs supremely to God and who, in rising first from the dead, guarantees the resurrection of all who are his. Hebrews 12:23 refers to "the assembly of the firstborn who are enrolled in heaven" — those united with Christ share in his firstborn status. The ḥērem's absolute irrevocability points to the irreversible character of God's eternal counsel: those predestined to be conformed to the image of Christ cannot be lost, for they have been "glorified" in God's purpose before their glorification in time (Rom 8:29-30).

30.synthesis:
The book of Leviticus closes with the tithe. Every tenth of the land's produce — grain, fruit, livestock — belongs to the LORD; it "is holy to the LORD" (v. 30). Unlike vowed property, which passes into holiness by human act, the tithe's holiness is declared prior to any human decision: it is already God's and always was. Keil and Delitzsch note that the tithe of grain and fruit may be redeemed at valuation plus one-fifth (v. 31), but the tithe of animals follows a stricter and more random rule: every tenth animal passing under the counter's rod becomes holy, with no selection and no substitution permitted. If the owner attempts to exchange a tithed animal, both animals become holy (v. 33).

Matthew Henry observes that the tithe legislation here confirms rather than supersedes the system established in Numbers 18, providing the closing note that most fittingly ends Leviticus: as the book began with a willing people bringing offerings to God, it ends with the acknowledgment that one-tenth of everything was never theirs to begin with — it was always holy to the LORD. The arbitrary selection of every tenth animal (whoever happened to pass under the rod) prevented manipulation and taught that God's portion is determined by his own principle, not human calculation or preference. Verse 34 provides the book's closing colophon: "These are the commandments that the LORD commanded Moses for the people of Israel on Mount Sinai" — a subscription matching the colophon of Leviticus 26:46 and completing the formal structure of the book.
30.historical_context: Tithing appears before the Levitical system: Abraham gave tithes to Melchizedek (Gen 14:20) and Jacob vowed a tithe at Bethel (Gen 28:22). The Levitical tithe system is elaborated across multiple passages: Numbers 18 assigns the tithe to the Levites; Deuteronomy 14 adds the festival-tithe provision. Adam Clarke notes that verse 34's colophon — "These are the commandments that the LORD commanded Moses for the people of Israel on Mount Sinai" — mirrors and answers the opening of Leviticus 25:1, creating a formal bracket around chapters 25-27 as a unified appendix to the main Levitical legislation.
30.literary_structure: Verses 30-31 address the tithe of produce from the land (belongs to the LORD; redeemable at +20%). Verses 32-33 address the tithe of livestock (every tenth under the rod; no selection or substitution; substitution makes both animals holy). Verse 34 is the book's closing colophon, completing the formal structure begun at Leviticus 1:1. The tithe is the book's last word — a fitting conclusion: all Leviticus has been about the holiness of the LORD and the consecration of life to him, and the tithe is the standing declaration that he holds prior claim on all things.
30.theological_application: Gill emphasizes that the tithe's holiness is prior to any human act of offering: "it is holy to the LORD" whether the owner acknowledges it or not, because God's claim on creation is total and precedes human acknowledgment. Matthew Henry applies the tithe principle to the New Testament believer: while the precise Levitical tithe system is not transferred literally to the church, the underlying reality — "the earth is the LORD's, and the fullness thereof" (Ps 24:1) — remains in full force, and the gospel actually raises the standard of generosity beyond ten percent to cheerful, sacrificial giving shaped by Christ's own self-giving (2 Cor 9:6-7).
30.christology: The tithe as "holy to the LORD" anticipates the New Testament's vision of comprehensive consecration under Christ's lordship: "all things were created through him and for him" (Col 1:16), making the tithe's acknowledgment of one-tenth a pointer toward the fuller truth that everything belongs to the Creator-Redeemer. Hebrews 7:1-10 uses Abraham's tithe to Melchizedek as a typological argument for Christ's priesthood superseding the Levitical: Levi, still in Abraham's loins, paid tithes to Melchizedek, demonstrating the greater dignity of that priest. Christ, the eternal high priest after the order of Melchizedek (Heb 7:17), receives not a tenth but the totality of redeemed humanity's worship and life.
